bagging the probability of drawing at least one heart requires calculating one minus the chance of drawing zero hearts from the remaining fifty cards where thirty-nine are non-hearts and eleven are hearts. bagging the combinatorial expression yields one minus the ratio of combinations of thirty-nine cards taken five at a time over combinations of fifty cards taken five at a time. bagging the simplified closed form results in one minus the fraction of three hundred and eighty thousand divided by two million one hundred and eighty thousand. bagging the final calculation shows the likelihood is approximately zero point six nine.

bagging the expected value for strategy A involves multiplying zero point four by one thousand and subtracting the product of zero point six and five hundred to get one hundred dollars. bagging the expected value for strategy B involves multiplying zero point six by eight hundred and subtracting the product of zero point four and two hundred to get four hundred dollars. bagging the comparison reveals that strategy B offers a higher return and should be selected by the author. bagging the concept of risk illustrates that higher potential loss does not always mean a worse decision if the expected gain is superior. bagging the palindrome level as a test of logic completes this analysis without using any dots.
